steps.title: Spot the decisive clue
steps.content: The parlor is called Tía Ana's “special province”—where she holds her little school. Yet Tía Ana has “already come into the room several times” checking if guests have left. This contrast between her ownership and her repeated checking is the key.

steps.title: Match to the choices
steps.content: The “special province” reference directly explains her behavior: the parlor is her school, but Salomé's salon now uses it every evening, making her repeatedly check for guests to leave. The others either mischaracterize her as feeling betrayed (A), wrongly attribute joy despite “never in order” (B), or describe unsupported escape-seeking (C). That matches choice D — D is correct.
